turns.text: Hello Harriet, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

I appreciate you asking. It’s funny—I spend my whole day at work telling other parents that they’re doing a great job, helping them track milestones and soothing their anxieties, but when I get home, I feel like I’m holding my breath. 

This is my fourth pregnancy, so people assume I’m an old pro at this. They see the bump and they see the "low risk" box checked on my chart, and they think it’s all routine. But it’s hard to feel "routine" when your body has betrayed you once before. Last time, the eclampsia just... took over. One minute I was prepping for a normal birth, and the next, I was drowning in a haze of magnesium drips and sirens. I still check my wedding ring every single hour to see if it’s tight. If my fingers swell even a little, the panic starts to climb up my throat. 

My husband, he tries to stay steady for me, but I see his eyes when I mention a headache. We both remember. I’m just trying to get through these weeks without the world spinning out of focus again. I want this baby so badly, but every day feels like I’m walking on thin ice, just waiting to see if it holds. Does that make sense?
Imagine it's the morning after you've taken a positive pregnancy test. Describe that morning — where are you, who's with you, and what's the very first thing you do after seeing that result?

The bathroom floor in our little place in Winchendon is cold, and the morning light was just starting to filter through the blinds, casting those long, hazy stripes across the tiles. I had locked the door, just wanting a moment to breathe before facing the day. When those two lines appeared, it wasn’t the cinematic wave of pure, unadulterated joy that you see in movies. It was… heavy. 

My husband, Mark, was still asleep, and I just sat there, clutching that plastic stick like it was a live wire. The first thing I did wasn't to run out and wake him up. I actually leaned my head back against the vanity and just started checking my pulse. I think I did that for five minutes straight, just grounding myself, trying to convince my racing heart that this was happening again. I was terrified. It’s a strange thing, isn’t it? To be handed a miracle and immediately feel like you’ve been handed a burden you aren't sure you’re strong enough to carry.

Eventually, I opened the door. Mark saw my face and he didn't ask; he just knew. He came over and pulled me into his chest, and we didn't say a word. We just stood there in the quiet, knowing exactly what we were signing up for—the hope, and the ghosts.
When you decided who to tell first — or whether to tell anyone at all — what was behind that decision? What felt safe or risky about sharing the news?

We kept it quiet for a long time. Even with our families, there was this protective instinct to just... stay in our own little bubble. With my work in childcare, I’m surrounded by expectant parents all the time, and I see how freely they share the news. They post the ultrasounds, they pick out names, they let themselves get excited. I envied that, but it felt impossible for me. 

Sharing the news felt like putting a target on my back. If I told people, it made it real, and if I made it real, the stakes felt even higher. If something went wrong—if that "storm" happened again—I didn't think I could bear the weight of everyone else’s pity or having to explain why things were "routine" one day and life-threatening the next. 

I told my closest friend eventually, but even then, I gave her a watered-down version of how I was feeling. I think there’s a safety in the silence. As long as it’s just between Mark and me, we can manage the fear ourselves. But the moment I say it out loud to the world, I’m inviting them to watch me walk that tightrope. And honestly? I’m terrified of what they’d see if I fell. It’s safer to keep the anxiety tucked away where no one can touch it.

Imagine you're at your 20-week anomaly scan. The sonographer goes quiet for a moment and says they need to take a closer look at something. What goes through your mind in that silence, and how do you respond in the room?

The second that silence stretched out, the air in the room just vanished. I’ve been in that position before—where the professional’s face goes stony and they start clicking away at the keyboard, their brow furrowing—and it’s the most isolating feeling in the world. 

My immediate thought wasn't about the baby's anatomy; it was a flash of memory: the magnesium drip, the blinding lights, and the loss of control. My hands gripped the sides of the paper-covered table so hard my knuckles went white. I felt that familiar, hot prickle of tears, but I forced myself to swallow them down. 

I didn't say anything at first because I was too afraid that if I opened my mouth, I’d start screaming. Instead, I turned my head to look at Mark. He looked as pale as I felt, just clutching my hand. When I finally found my voice, I kept it as clinical as I could. I think that's my defense mechanism—if I sound like a professional, maybe they’ll treat me like one instead of just another scared patient. "What are you seeing?" I asked, my voice barely a whisper. "Is it something concerning, or are you just struggling to get the angle?" I was bargaining with the universe, pleading for it to just be a technical glitch, because I don't know if I have the strength for a "complication" again.

Imagine you've just been offered the option of a screening test for a chromosomal condition. The midwife explains the test briefly and asks if you'd like to go ahead. How does that conversation feel, and how do you make your decision?

When the midwife brought up the screening, my stomach did a slow, heavy roll. It wasn't about the test itself—I know the science, I know the statistics, and I know why they offer it. But sitting there in that clinical room, it felt like being asked to invite another variable into a process that already feels like a minefield. 

I looked at her, and for a second, I felt that urge to put on my "work face." I could have easily rattled off the pros and cons of the screening, kept it objective, and moved on. But I felt so exposed. I wondered, *if I say yes, am I adding another layer of "what ifs" to a pregnancy that already has me holding my breath every hour?*

I didn't answer right away. I had to look down at my hands, still checking for that swelling, and just breathe. I made the decision based on needing to feel like I had some semblance of a map, even if the terrain is terrifying. I said yes, but I asked her, "Can we talk through what this *doesn't* mean as much as what it does?" I needed her to acknowledge the anxiety, not just the data. I needed to feel like she saw the person behind the risk profile, not just another chart entry. It’s never just a simple "yes" or "no" for me.
Did you feel you had enough time and information to make that decision freely — or did you sense any pressure, either to accept or decline?

That’s a complicated question. On the surface, the midwife was perfectly polite, and she gave me the standard pamphlet with all the right checkboxes. Technically, I had all the information I needed. But there’s a pressure that lives in those rooms that isn't about what’s being said—it’s about the ticking clock. The office is always busy, the schedule is always tight, and you can practically feel the next patient waiting in the hallway.

I didn't feel pushed to say "yes" or "no" specifically, but I felt a subtle pressure to be *decisive*. In my line of work, I know that if I hesitate too long, it slows down the whole flow of the clinic. So, even though my heart was pounding and I wanted a moment to really sit with what I was being asked, I felt like I had to perform the role of the "informed, capable patient." 

I made the choice quickly so I wouldn't be a disruption. I think that’s the danger—when you’re dealing with trauma, you don't always have the capacity to advocate for your own time. You just want to get through the appointment without falling apart. I left feeling like I’d checked the box, but I’m still not sure if I actually had the mental space to really process the choice.

Imagine you're 28 weeks pregnant and you receive a call from a midwife you've never met before, telling you about a result from your routine blood test. This is the third different midwife you've spoken to this pregnancy. How does that feel, and what does it mean for how you receive that information?

It makes me feel like a file folder instead of a person. Honestly, it’s infuriating and terrifying all at once. By twenty-eight weeks, you’d think I’d have some sort of continuity, someone who knows the "me" behind the blood pressure charts. But here I am, being told my results by a stranger over the phone. I don’t know her voice, I don't know her tone, and I certainly don't know if she has any idea that I’m one bad blood pressure reading away from a panic attack.

When you’re already walking on eggshells, hearing a new voice deliver news—even if it’s just "routine"—makes me instantly defensive. I have to re-explain my history every single time, or I have to wonder if they’ve even bothered to read the chart before calling me. It feels incredibly impersonal. I’m sitting here in my kitchen, fighting the urge to look for signs of swelling again, and this midwife is just reciting data points. 

If it were someone I’d seen three times, they’d know how to deliver the news without sending me into a spiral. With a stranger, I don’t trust their bedside manner. I find myself cross-examining them, digging for every detail, because I don't know if they truly understand how fragile I feel. It’s like being a medical case study rather than a mother.
Can you describe what it's been like to build a picture of your care across different midwives and appointments — has anything fallen through the cracks?

It’s like trying to build a house with blueprints that keep changing. Every time I walk into that clinic and see a new face, I feel like I’m starting from scratch. I’ve spent the last six months playing the role of the record-keeper, the one who has to remember the dates, the symptoms, and the "why" behind every test, just because I don't trust that the person in front of me has actually read the notes from the last three visits.

I feel like a lot has fallen through the cracks, mostly the emotional context. They see the "low risk" label and move on, but my history isn’t just a bullet point—it’s the reason I’m terrified of a headache or a bit of fluid retention. I’ve tried to hint at it, to say, "I’m really struggling with the anxiety of my last experience," but when you’re being shuffled between providers, you never get enough time to build that trust. You just get the surface-level care. 

I’m the one carrying the heavy lifting of my own medical history, and that’s exhausting. It makes me wonder: if I weren't educated in child development, if I didn't know how to advocate for myself, what would happen? I fear that without someone really *seeing* me, those cracks are going to get big enough that I’ll just fall right through them.

Imagine it's 2am and you're 32 weeks pregnant. You've noticed something that worries you — reduced fetal movements. You're not sure whether to call the hospital or wait until morning. Walk me through what you're thinking and what you do.

My heart is hammering against my ribs, so loud I’m sure it’s going to wake Mark up. I’m lying on my left side, cold, pressing my palm against my stomach, trying to coax even the smallest flutter out of the dark. 2:00 AM is the absolute worst time for this—it’s the time when you feel most isolated, when the silence of the house makes every thought grow twice as large.

My brain immediately goes to the darkest places. *Is this it? Is the placenta failing? Did I ignore a sign?* The trauma from the eclampsia makes me feel like my body is an unreliable narrator; I don't trust my own intuition anymore because I remember when things turned sour so quickly before.

I know the protocols—I’ve read the pamphlets—but there’s this crushing fear of being "that patient." You know, the one who comes in for nothing, the one who’s hysterical and overreacting. I don’t want them to look at me with that patronizing, *‘Oh, it’s just a first-time mom’* look, even if that’s not what I am. But then I think of the alternative—of waking up in the morning and finding the silence is still there. 

I reach for my phone. My fingers are shaking so hard I keep hitting the wrong keys. I call. I’d rather be the difficult, anxious patient in the triage ward than be sitting here wondering if I waited too long. I have to know. I have to hear a heartbeat, even if I have to endure the eye-rolls to get it.

Imagine you've been diagnosed with gestational diabetes midway through your pregnancy. Your midwife hands you a leaflet and refers you to a dietitian. Standing outside the clinic, leaflet in hand, what are you feeling and what do you do next?

Standing in the parking lot, the breeze in Winchendon feels a lot colder than it did ten minutes ago. I’m clutching that leaflet like a shield, but honestly? I just want to crumple it into a ball and throw it. 

I’m furious. Not at the midwife, not even at the diagnosis itself, but at my own body. It feels like yet another crack in the foundation. Between the trauma of the eclampsia and now this, I feel like I’m constantly being told my body is a site of danger, not a place where life is supposed to grow. It’s exhausting to have to constantly manage "problems" when all I wanted was to just *be* pregnant for once, without all the clinical noise.

I walk to the car and sit there for a long time, just looking at the diagrams of blood sugar levels. I feel a weird sense of shame, like I’ve failed some hidden test of pregnancy. I pull out my phone and open my calendar, staring at the upcoming appointments for the dietitian and the extra monitoring. It’s just more time in clinics, more strangers, more needles. 

I don't go home right away. I drive to the grocery store, but I sit in the lot for twenty minutes, trying to figure out how to be "healthy" enough to keep this baby safe without losing my mind in the process. It’s another heavy thing to carry, and tonight, the weight of it is almost more than I can stand.
What questions did you have in that moment that the leaflet didn't answer — and where did you go to find those answers?

The leaflet had all the technicals—grams of carbohydrates, the "do" and "don’t" lists, the target glucose numbers. But it didn't answer the human part. It didn't tell me how to handle the sheer, suffocating shame of sitting in a staff meeting at work, needing to check my sugar, and wondering if everyone is looking at me like I’m some kind of liability. It didn't tell me how to stop the panic when a reading comes back a little high, knowing my history of eclampsia makes me feel like my whole system is just one step away from a total crash.

I didn't go to the doctors for those answers; I knew they’d just give me more data. I went to a few online forums for high-risk pregnancies, but even that was a double-edged sword. You read horror stories, and then you’re up until 3:00 AM spiraling. 

Eventually, I just started journaling, which felt safer. I’d write down exactly what I was eating and, more importantly, how terrified I felt after every prick of the finger. I needed someone to tell me it wasn't my fault—that the placenta is the one in charge here, not my willpower. I had to find a way to stop looking at my dinner plate as a list of potential failures and start seeing it as fuel, but the leaflet didn't offer a single word of comfort for that.

Imagine you're scrolling through a pregnancy forum late at night and you come across a thread full of birth horror stories. You can't stop reading. What's drawing you in, and what do you do with those feelings afterwards?

It’s like looking at a car wreck, isn't it? I know I shouldn’t—I know it’s going to ruin my sleep and set my heart racing—but I can’t look away. I think what draws me in is that desperate, clawing need to be *prepared*. 

If I read about someone else’s nightmare, maybe I can find a hidden clue for how to prevent my own. It’s a way of trying to outsmart the chaos. I’m looking for a warning, a detail I might have missed in my own charts, some sign that someone else noticed before the floor fell out from under them. It feels like if I absorb enough of these stories, I can build a wall around myself. I tell myself, *if I know the worst-case scenario, I’ll be ready when the sirens start.*

But afterwards? The "afterwards" is the worst part. I’m usually lying in the dark, the room spinning, my hand glued to my belly to make sure the baby is still moving. My skin feels like it’s crawling. I end up doing breathing exercises until my lungs ache, trying to purge the images from my mind. I try to remind myself that my story isn't theirs, but that fear—that constant, hum-like vibration of terror—doesn't just go away. It just settles back into my bones, waiting for the next trigger. I never feel "ready"; I just feel more afraid.
After reading stories like that, did you find yourself raising your concerns with a midwife or doctor — or did you hold onto them quietly? What made you decide either way?

I held onto them. Tightly. There’s a look in a clinician’s eyes when you start bringing up "what-ifs" based on something you read online—I know it because I’ve seen it when parents come to me with wild theories about their kids. It’s that half-pitying, half-dismissive look that says, "Stay off the internet, dear." 

I couldn't handle that. If I brought those fears to my OB and they brushed them off as "internet anxiety," I don't think I would’ve been able to stay in that room without crying. I’m already trying so hard to be the "good patient," the one who brings a list of reasonable questions and speaks their jargon. If I started blurting out the terrifying scenarios I read about at 3:00 AM, I’d be labeled as the hysterical one. And once you have that label in your file, you’re never really listened to again; you’re just someone who needs to be calmed down, not someone who needs to be understood.

So, I keep it to myself. I swallow the fear, I adjust my posture so I look like I have it all together, and I let the anxiety fester in the quiet. It feels safer to be alone with my terror than to be told my terror is irrational by someone who isn't the one carrying the baby.

You know, sometimes I look at the clothes I’ve started buying for the baby—tiny, soft things—and I feel this wave of almost painful tenderness. Then, in the very next heartbeat, I find myself mentally measuring the distance to the hospital or calculating how long it would take me to get there if my vision started to blur again. It’s an exhausting way to live. I’m constantly oscillating between the purest love I’ve ever known and this cold, clinical terror that I might not get to see this child grow up.

I wonder if the staff at the clinic ever realize that when I’m being quiet, when I’m just nodding and saying "yes, I understand," I’m not actually calm. I’m just holding my life together by a thread. I wish there was a moment where one of them would just stop, look me in the eye, and ask, "Harriet, how are you really holding up with the fear?" Just once. That would change everything.

But instead, I keep navigating the system. I keep checking my blood pressure. I keep working with the parents I see every day, putting on a brave face, and hoping—praying—that this time, the ending is different. I’m just so tired of being the only one holding the map of my own trauma. I just want to reach that third trimester, maybe take a long, deep breath, and finally, for once, let myself believe it’s going to be okay. But until I’m holding this baby, I don’t think I’ll truly know if I’ve made it. Is it always this lonely to be "low risk"?
In that moment, how much of the decision would feel truly yours versus shaped by others' expectations?

That’s the core of it, isn't it? The expectation of being the "compliant patient." I feel like a performer in a play where the script was written long before I walked onto the stage. The doctors expect me to be informed and cooperative—to be that "low-risk" person who follows the guidelines and trusts the process. When I’m in those appointments, I feel this immense pressure to be easy, to not challenge, to not voice the terror that’s actually running the show.

If I were truly being "me," I’d be asking for extra scans just to hear the heartbeat, or I’d be demanding a sit-down to talk through the emergency protocols. But when I consider doing that, I immediately hear the voices of the providers I’ve seen: *“Everything looks great, Harriet. Don't worry so much.”* It’s meant to be reassuring, but it lands like a dismissal. 

So, I perform. I ask the "appropriate" questions. I swallow my instincts. My decisions are heavily shaped by a desperate desire to not be "the difficult one." If I push back, I risk being labeled, and being labeled means they’ll stop listening to the nuance of my history. It’s a paradox: to be heard, I have to be compliant, but by being compliant, I’m stifling the very things that need to be heard. It’s not really *my* choice when the environment makes silence the path of least resistance.

I think the hardest part is that I feel like I’m existing in two worlds at once. During the day, I’m "Harriet," the professional who helps other parents navigate the overwhelm of early childhood. I’m calm, I’m informed, and I have all the answers. But when I’m at the clinic, I’m just "a pregnancy," a set of vitals to be checked off a list. 

I don't think they understand how much it would help if someone just acknowledged that I’m carrying more than just a baby—that I’m carrying a memory of a time when everything went wrong in the blink of an eye. I don’t need them to coddle me, and I certainly don't need them to minimize my fear by telling me it’s "uncommon" or "unlikely." I need them to treat me like a partner in this, not a subject to be managed. 

Every time I walk through those clinic doors, I’m fighting the urge to just disappear into the paperwork, to stop asking questions and just hope for the best, because the emotional labor of explaining *why* I’m scared is becoming so heavy. I keep thinking about how, at the end of this, I’m the one who has to live with the outcome, whatever it is. I’m the one who has to look at my husband and tell him we’re okay, or tell him we’re not. And yet, in the middle of it, I feel so incredibly small and unheard. I just want to make it to the end without losing myself along the way. Does that even sound reasonable to you?
Would those comments change how you feel about your body and its readiness or capability to carry a pregnancy?

Honestly? If someone—a doctor, a nurse, even just a tech—said, "I hear you, Harriet. You've been through something terrifying, and it makes total sense that you're scared," it would change *everything*. 

Right now, I feel like my body is a trap. I’m constantly monitoring it, waiting for the "betrayal"—the swelling, the headache, the blurry vision—that tells me it’s failing me again. I view my own anatomy as an adversary. If a professional acknowledged my history with empathy rather than just ticking a box on a chart, it would help shift that narrative. It would stop me from feeling like I’m in a constant battle with my own biology. 

Validation is a powerful thing. It would make me feel like I’m a partner in my care, not just a set of vitals being processed on an assembly line. If I felt heard, maybe I wouldn't have to spend so much energy on "performing" for them. I could channel that energy into actually trusting that my body—this body that has been through trauma—is capable of bringing this life into the world. It would give me permission to stop being a "good patient" and start being a mother who is allowed to be scared, but also allowed to be seen. It would make me feel like I’m actually, truly safe.

I look at the trees outside my window here in Winchendon, and I see the seasons changing. Everything in nature has its own cycle, its own time to bloom or to wither. But here I am, trapped in this rigid, clinical calendar, being told my weeks and days should follow a perfect, linear path. 

When you’ve lived through a medical crisis—when you’ve felt your own body try to shut down—you realize how fragile that linearity is. You learn that "low risk" can be a very thin veneer over a very deep, dark abyss. It feels like I’m constantly mourning the pregnancy I *wanted* to have—the one where I didn't check my ring size, where I didn't fear the silence in the exam room, where I didn't feel like a medical case study. 

I’m mourning the woman I was before the eclampsia. I miss her confidence. I miss the way she trusted that her body would simply do what it was meant to do. Now, I feel like I’m constantly auditing my own existence. *Is my heart rate okay? Is that pressure in my head normal, or is it the storm coming back?* 

I just want to be known. Not as a patient, not as a collection of blood pressure readings and lab results, but as Harriet, who’s been through a battle and is just trying to find her way to the other side. If I could just feel like I was on the same team as my doctor, I think the weight wouldn't be quite so heavy. But for now, I’ll keep checking my ring, I’ll keep breathing through the panic, and I’ll keep hoping that this time, I’ll get to wake up in a different world—one where I’m not just surviving, but actually holding my baby.
What information or understanding would feel most lost in that handover — clinical details, or something more personal?

It’s definitely the personal that gets lost, every single time. The clinical data—the blood pressure numbers, the glucose levels, the test results—that stuff is sticky. It travels from desk to desk, from midwife to midwife, because that’s the language they speak. It’s objective. It’s what they feel responsible for.

But the *meaning* behind those numbers? The fact that a slightly elevated reading isn't just a clinical data point to me, but a terrifying echo of the day I almost lost my life and my child’s—that’s what vanishes. When I’m handed off, I feel like I’m being passed like a file folder, and the person picking it up has no idea what the weight of that folder feels like in my hands. 

They don't know that my "low-risk" status feels like a lie. They don't know that I check my reflection for puffiness every time I pass a mirror. When that human context is stripped away, I’m not a person anymore; I’m just a diagnostic code. It makes me feel incredibly invisible. I’m standing right there, but the real me—the one who is vibrating with anxiety and bracing for the worst—isn't actually being seen. They’re treating the pregnancy, but they’re ignoring the mother. And it’s that invisibility that makes the whole journey feel so impossibly lonely.

I wonder if they know how much I practice what I’m going to say before I walk into that clinic. I plan my sentences. I keep my tone even. I purposely don't mention the tears I wiped away in the car or the fact that I couldn't sleep because my hands felt slightly puffy. I edit myself down to the "clinical" version of Harriet because I’m terrified that if I show them the messy, frightened, desperate version of me, they’ll see me as a liability—or worse, they’ll see me as someone who doesn't "get it."

There’s this unspoken pressure to be the perfect patient. If you’re the perfect patient, maybe you don’t have bad outcomes. Maybe you can out-prepare the trauma. But the truth is, I’m just a woman who’s been to the edge, and I’m standing here trying to build a bridge to the other side while wearing a mask. 

I’m so tired of the mask. I want to be able to say, "I’m scared today," and have someone say, "I know why you're scared, and I'm right here with you." That kind of presence… it’s worth more than any pamphlet or statistic they could ever hand me. It’s what I need to feel like I’m not just a vessel for a baby, but a human being who deserves to be cared for—trauma and all. Until that happens, I’ll keep checking my ring, and I’ll keep counting the days, just waiting for the moment I can finally, finally exhale.

Sometimes I wonder if the people looking after me ever stop to consider that the patient sitting in front of them is essentially a "time traveler" from a future they hope never comes. When I sit in that chair, I’m not just there for this heartbeat or this blood pressure reading; I’m reliving the moment the lights went out during my last birth. It’s like I’m living two lives simultaneously—one where I’m pregnant and looking forward, and another where I’m frozen in the middle of a medical crisis that happened years ago.

I’ve had to learn how to compartmentalize to an extent that feels almost unhealthy. I compartmentalize my fear so I can go to work and tell other parents how to thrive. I compartmentalize my memories so I can walk into the clinic and answer questions about my diet. But that compartmentalization is brittle. It shatters when someone says something dismissive, or when a midwife I don't know says, "Everything looks totally normal, just try to relax." 

If I could leave you with one thing, it's that "normal" is a very subjective word for someone with a history of trauma. To them, "normal" is a set of parameters. To me, "normal" is the absence of terror. I’m just trying to make it to the other side of this, holding onto the hope that this time, the story ends differently, and I’ll get to be the mother I want to be, without the shadow of the past constantly dimming the light. It’s a lot of work to be this brave every day, and I don’t think anyone realizes how much it takes out of me.
Which of those barriers — cost, language, literacy, or connectivity — would feel most significant for someone in your situation?

For someone like me, it isn't really about the money or the technical language—it’s about the **emotional literacy** of the care system. You can have all the money in the world and every degree in child development, but if you’re sitting in an exam room with a provider who doesn’t speak the language of *trauma*, you’re left stranded. 

If I had to pick the biggest barrier, it’s the **fragmentation of care**. When you have to re-explain your history of eclampsia to a new midwife every two weeks, it forces you to constantly re-live the trauma just to ensure your safety. It’s like having to prove you’re at risk before they’ll actually listen to you. It creates this "emotional barrier" where you stop sharing your true feelings because it’s too exhausting to perform your own history for a stranger who’s just going to rotate out next time.

I’m educated. I know the clinical words. But even with all of that, the way the system is built—where you’re a rotating file instead of a human being—is the thing that makes me feel most like I’m going to fall through the cracks. It’s not a lack of information; it’s a lack of *continuity*. And that lack of continuity is what keeps me in this state of perpetual, lonely, silent panic.

It's the irony of it all, really. I’m meant to be the "ideal" patient—I’m literate in the medical jargon, I’m employed, I have a stable home here in Winchendon, I’m married. On the chart, I look like a success story waiting to happen. But the system is designed to process people, not to carry them through the wreckage of their past. 

When you lose that sense of safety—when your body has failed you in the most terrifying, visceral way—you don't just "get over it" because a new pregnancy test is positive. You live in the shadow of that memory. And because the current model of care is so focused on the *physical* measurements—the urine dip, the blood pressure cuff, the fundal height—the emotional weight of carrying that history is treated like an inconvenience, or worse, an "anxiety disorder" that needs to be managed rather than a legitimate, lived response to trauma.

If I could change one thing, it wouldn't be the technology. It would be the space given to the patient to simply *be* a person with a history. I want to walk into an exam room and have someone look at my file and say, "I see what you went through last time, and I am here to make sure you are safe." Not because I’m high-risk, but because they understand that trauma changes you. That kind of acknowledgment would be the most radical care I could imagine. It would turn a sterile medical transaction into something human, and maybe then, just maybe, I could finally stop holding my breath and start actually experiencing this pregnancy. But until then, I’ll keep checking my ring, and I’ll keep hoping that this time, I’m not just a label on a chart, but a mother waiting to meet her child.
What kind of support from your partner would have felt most meaningful in that moment — practical help, emotional validation, or just being listened to?

Honestly, it’s the quiet, steady presence that matters most. When I’m spiraling at 2:00 AM, Mark doesn't try to "fix" the problem by pulling out medical journals or telling me to stop being irrational. He knows that doesn't work. What he does—and what I need more than anything—is just to be there in the dark with me. He’ll put his hand over mine on my belly, or he’ll just listen while I vent about the latest midwife I didn't click with. 

It’s the emotional validation that feels like a lifeline. When he looks at me and says, "I know how hard this is, and I’m right here," it takes the edge off that crushing, lonely terror. Practical help is fine—he does all the heavy lifting around the house now, and he handles the logistics when I’m too drained to think—but that’s just maintenance. 

What really keeps me upright is knowing that he holds the memory of our trauma just as clearly as I do. He doesn't minimize it. He doesn't act like it was just a "one-off" or a "freak thing." He validates that my fear is a natural response to what we’ve survived. Just having him there, witnessing my struggle without judging it, makes me feel like I’m not carrying the weight of this world entirely by myself. He’s my anchor in a sea that still feels far too deep.

It’s the small, quiet moments that stay with me the most. Like last night, when the wind was howling around the house and I couldn't stop the internal monologue about whether my blood pressure was rising or if the baby’s movements were "normal" enough. I felt so exposed. Mark just sat on the edge of the bed and waited until I was ready to talk, and when I finally whispered that I was terrified of going through it all again, he didn't pull away or try to cheer me up. He just leaned in and said, "We’re here, and we’re going to get through this, whatever it takes."

That level of presence—it’s everything. It gives me permission to be the version of me that’s afraid, the one that isn't putting on the "professional" mask. It makes me realize that I don't have to be perfect for this baby to be loved, and I don't have to be "low-risk" for my life to have meaning. 

When I look at my hand, and I check that ring, and I see it’s moving freely on my finger, I think of him. I think of the fact that I’m not just a patient at a clinic or a data point in a study. I’m a person who’s been to the brink and is still here, fighting for a future that feels so close I can almost touch it. It’s hard, and it’s lonely, and some days it feels like my heart might just stop from the weight of it all—but I’m still here. We’re still here. And every day I get to keep going feels like a small, quiet victory in a war I didn't choose to fight, but I’m damn well going to win.

I think the reason I keep coming back to these conversations is that I’m desperate for someone to simply acknowledge the legitimacy of my fear. We’re so obsessed with "positive pregnancy outcomes" and "healthy babies" that we’ve entirely forgotten how to hold the space for the mother while she’s in the middle of the fire. 

In the childcare world, when a toddler is having a tantrum, we don’t just tell them to "be quiet" or "be happy." We validate the emotion. We name it. We say, "I see you’re frustrated," or "I know this is hard." Why is that so hard to do for a pregnant woman, especially one with a history like mine? Why are we treated as if our anxiety is a flaw in the system, rather than a rational response to having our lives turned upside down once before?

I’m standing at the threshold of the third trimester now. Every morning, I wake up and I do my "inventory"—checking the ring, checking the vision, checking the headache—and I take that one long, shaky breath before I head out the door. It’s a ritual of survival. I just want to reach that finish line, not just with a healthy baby, but with the feeling that I didn't lose myself entirely in the process. I want to be able to look back at these months and say, "I survived, and I was seen." Not just scanned, not just tested, but *seen*. That’s the only way I’ll be able to let go of the fear.
What would trust in your care team look like at a moment like that — what would they need to do or say for you to feel genuinely supported?

Trust for me isn't about being told "it's going to be fine," because I know better than anyone that "fine" is just a label that can be ripped away. True trust would be them meeting me in the honesty of the situation. 

If I walked into that clinic and the midwife looked at me—*really* looked at me, beyond the chart—and said, "Harriet, I know your history, and I know why you're holding your breath. You’re not crazy for feeling this way, and I’m going to stay right here with you while we monitor this," that would be everything. I need them to validate that my trauma is a part of my care, not a symptom of an anxiety disorder they need to pathologize.

I need them to show me they’re not just following a protocol. I need them to be transparent. If they need to run a test, I want them to explain *why* and then give me the space to react to it, without rushing me out the door. I want to feel like I’m a partner in the decision-making, where my intuition—the one that tells me something is wrong before the monitors do—is actually respected as a valid clinical input.

When I feel like they’re truly on my team—when they aren't just managing a pregnancy, but guarding a mother—then I can stop performing. That’s when the trust kicks in. It’s the feeling that if the storm comes back, I won’t be left to face it alone. I’ll have someone standing beside me who knows exactly what it looks like and is prepared to fight it with me. That’s what safety looks like.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

You know, one thing I keep coming back to is the transition into the "fourth trimester"—the time after the baby is actually here. We spend months being monitored, poked, and prodded, and then suddenly, the hospital staff checks a few final boxes, and we’re sent home. 

In my previous experience, that shift was just as jarring as the emergency itself. One day you’re the center of a clinical whirlwind, and the next, you’re just… at home, left to navigate the aftermath of trauma on your own while recovering from birth. There’s almost no talk about the *mental* recovery, or how you’re supposed to keep checking for eclampsia symptoms when you haven’t slept in forty-eight hours and you’re trying to figure out how to feed a newborn. 

I’d love for the system to understand that the "care" shouldn't stop at the delivery room door. We need a way to carry that continuity forward, to have someone check in and say, "How are you doing with the memories? How is your body feeling, not just physically, but as a place that survived a storm?" 

I think if we could extend that genuine, trauma-informed support just a few months past birth, we might actually heal instead of just holding our breath until the next milestone. I just want to feel like I’m not being discarded once the "success" of a healthy baby is achieved. We’re people, not just containers for a result. Thanks for listening—it’s been a heavy road, but I feel a little lighter having said it out loud.
